coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorescent carbon dots derived from tamarind
coredata.dc:creator: Asha Jhonsi, Mariadoss
Thulasi, Sekar
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel fluorescent carbon dots (TCDs) is synthesized from tamarind by a simple one pot hydrothermal method. The synthesized TCDs are completely characterized by vital techniques. TCDs exhibit an excitation-dependent emission with the quantum yield of 4% and storage stability of more than three months without any stabilizer. Further, fluorescence quenching of TCDs by acceptors such as methyl viologen and hydroxy TEMPO are studied. Quenching of TCDs is due to electron transfer as evidenced by the decrease in fluorescence lifetime measurements.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
Tamarind
Fluorescent carbon dots
Methyl viologen
TCSPC

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: M. A. J and S. T thanks to DST-SERB, India for the project and fellowship respectively [Ref. No. SB/FT/CS-125/2013, Dt. 30/06/2014]. M.A.J thanks Nano-IITM and SAIF, IIT Madras for assistance with HR-TEM and SEM facilities. M.A.J. also acknowledges Prof. G.U. Kulkarni, Ms. Selvi and Ms. Kiruthika for EDAS and power XRD analysis. We also thanks to National Centre for Ultrafast processes, University of Madras for lifetime measurements. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image at 50nm scale, insert: TEM image at 5μm scale, (b) SEM image, (c) EDAS analysis and (d) XRD patterns of TCDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, (b) the FTIR spectrum, (c) excitation dependent emission spectra and (d) fluorescence decay of TCDs.

(a) Time resolved fluorescence decay of TCDs with varying methyl viologen concentration (0–5× 10−3 M) λexi =375nm and (b) Stern-Volmer plot for the quenching of TCDs by methyl viologen and hydroxyl TEMPO.
Proposed mechanism for the formation of TCDs from tamarind.

head.ce:title: A novel fluorescent carbon dots derived from tamarind

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, B.S. Abdur Rahman University, Chennai 600 048, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel fluorescent carbon dots (TCDs) is synthesized from tamarind by a simple one pot hydrothermal method. The synthesized TCDs are completely characterized by vital techniques. TCDs exhibit an excitation-dependent emission with the quantum yield of 4% and storage stability of more than three months without any stabilizer. Further, fluorescence quenching of TCDs by acceptors such as methyl viologen and hydroxy TEMPO are studied. Quenching of TCDs is due to electron transfer as evidenced by the decrease in fluorescence lifetime measurements.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past one decade there has been much more effort in the research community towards the searching of an alternative for organic fluorophores and heavy metal based semiconductor quantum dots for diverse applications [1]. Carbon dots are generally oxygenous carbon nanoparticles with a size of less than 10nm which exhibits the properties as similar to the semiconductor nanoparticles [2]. They have been a promising alternative owing to their properties such as high water solubility, low cytotoxicity, biocompatibility, high emission quantum yield, low cost of production and high photo and storage stability [1,3]. Moreover, carbon dots are widely used in multidisciplinary fields such as optical, optoelectronic and biological applications such as photocatalysis, photovoltaics, anode material in quantum dots solar cells for electron shuttling, bioimaging, biomarking, drug delivery and photodynamic therapy [4,5].

In general carbon quantum dots can be synthesised by both top-down (laser ablation [6], electro deposition [7,8] arc discharge [9], etc.) and bottom-up (precipitation [10], microwave [11], hydro and solvothermal [12], etc.) approaches. Among the various physical and chemical synthetic methods reported for the synthesis of carbon based materials, the hydrothermal method is the best among the other due to its one pot, simple, low temperature and economical advantages [13]. A large number of reports available for both the chemical as well as green synthesis of carbon dots from various sources like graphitic oxide, natural gas and candle soot, citrate, ascorbic acid, chicken eggs, chitoson, gelatine, fruit extracts, vegetables, green leaves, flower extracts, food wastes, honey, hair, sugar, ginger, coffee ground, etc. [14].

Yin Wang et al. reported the photoinduced electron transfer of carbon dots with nitro compounds in which the quenching of fluorescence emission in the carbon dots can occur by both electron donors and electron acceptors. In that they have reported that surface passivation of carbon dots is responsible for the quenching mechanism [15]. Sahu and coworkers reported that the one step synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots with the quantum yield of 26% from orange juice towards the application bio-imaging [12]. Similarly, Yue et al. reported that carbon quantum dots from apple juice for the detection of metal ion like Hg2+ present in water sample collected from various sources likely carbon quantum dots as a sensor for metal ions [13]. In another work, Wang and co workers mentioned that among the variable green vegetable extracts, carbon dots derived from spinach (a green leaf) exhibits favourable photoluminescence characteristics and amine functionalization passivate the surface well. In that they have synthesized, water soluble carbon dots as well as nanocomposites of TiO2 with carbon dots via green route towards the application of photocatalytic hydrogen production [16]. Recently, Lim and his co worker reported a detailed review on carbon quantum dots and their applications. In that review, they have collected the most possible reports available in the literature which related to the carbon dots and their fluorescence properties, synthetic methods and application areas [12].

Although carbon based materials derived from various precursors for wide variety of applications have been reported, to the best of our knowledge, the direct synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from tamarind and its properties are remains not yet reported. The selection of the precursor plays a key role in the production yield and properties of carbon dots, which makes them as potential candidates for diverse applications. So it is believed that if the source contains citric acid or ascorbic acid in higher percentage which exhibits good enough quantum yield [12,17,18]. Tamarind, a daily use Indian kitchen material have 4 and 9% of citric acid and tartaric acid respectively [19]. Therefore, in the present work we are reporting the synthesis and complete characterization CDs from tamarind. Electron transfer quenching of TCDs with electron acceptors has also been demonstrated.

Seed removed tamarind is purchased from local supermarket. Methyl viologen dichloride hydrate (98%) and 4-Hydroxy-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine 1-oxyl (hydroxy TEMPO) (97%) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich and used as such without further purification. Other chemicals and solvents were of analytical grade and purchased from LOBA Chemicals (India). Milli-Q water was used to prepare the samples for spectral measurements.

X-ray powder diffraction patterns were recorded on a Rigaku MiniFleXll-C system using Cu Kα radiation (λ =1.5418Å) radiation and a graphite monochromator in the diffracted beam. A scan rate of 1°min−1 was applied to record a pattern in the 2θ =25° in the range of 2θ =20–80°. Particle size and morphology of the synthesized TCDs were investigated by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2100 with accelerating voltage of 120kV). The TEM specimen was prepared by depositing a few drops of a diluted carbon dot solution onto a carbon-coated copper grid, followed by complete evaporation of the water solvent. Diameter of TCDs is calculated by using Image J software which resulting the area of particles present. A=π r2, r=√(A/π) and D=2× r. In this A is area of particles occupied, r is radius of particles and D is diameter of the particles. Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) analysis is done by using Agilent 5500. The AFM specimen on a mica surface was prepared as similar to TEM analysis and the non-contact mode was used for the AFM image measurement. SEM measurement is done by using Nova NanoSEM 600 from FEI Company, Netherlands and EDS analysis is with EDAX system. Elemental analysis for the dried TCDs sample is done by Perkin Elmer 2400 CHN analyser. The diameter of the particles, self aggregation behaviour and surface charge is determined by dynamic light scattering method by using Malvern DLS instrument and zeta potential measurement respectively. FT-IR spectra were obtained by using JASCO FT–IR ATR 6300 spectrometer at room temperature in the range of 4000–400cm−1. The samples were placed in a liquid cell between two windows (CaF2). Mirror velocity is 0.3cms−1 and numbers of co-added scans are 4 then total collection times is less than 2min. Absorption spectra were recorded using Perkin Elmer Lamda 25 UV–visible spectrophotometer. The fluorescence quenching measurements were carried out with Perkin Elmer LS 45 spectrofluorometer. The excitation and emission slit width (each 5nm) and scan rate (200nmmin−1) were maintained constant for all the measurements. Time-resolved fluorescence decays were obtained by the time correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) technique exciting the sample at 375nm. Data analysis was carried out by the software provided by IBH (DAS-6), which is based on deconvolution a technique using nonlinear least-squares method.

The quantum yield (ϕ) of TCDs was calculated by using quinine sulphate as a reference compound in which ϕ value is reported as 0.53 [24]. For the measurement of ϕ, the optical density of TCDs in water (η =1.33) was fixed to less than 0.1 at the wavelength of 360nm. Quinine sulphate is dissolved in 0.1M H2SO4 (η =1.33). For both the sample and reference, emission spectra were recorded at the same excitation at 360nm. Fluorescence quantum yield (ϕ F) for TCDs was calculated by using following Eq. (1)
(1)
ϕ
F
= (
A
R
/
A
S
) (
I
S
/
I
R
)
(
η
S
/
η
R
)
2
ϕ
R
where the subscript ‘S’ refers to the samples, the subscript ‘R’ refers to quinine sulphate, A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, I is the integrated emission area and η is the solvent refraction index.

Carbon dots from tamarind is synthesized by a simple one pot hydrothermal method. A typical procedure for the synthesis is as follows, 10g of seed removed tamarind is placed in a 100ml Teflon coated autoclave. 50ml of Millipore water is added into that and tightly closed and kept in an electrical furnace at 180°C for 8h, then allows the autoclave to cool to room temperature naturally. A dark brown crude solution is recovered from the autoclave and centrifuged at 2000rpm for 10min to remove the larger bulk particles. A clear yellow solution is collected and filtered through an ultra filtration membrane (0.2μm) and washed with dichloromethane in order to remove the unreacted organic moieties present in the solution. The washing is repeated for minimum of three times using a separating funnel and finally the tamarind carbon dots (TCDs) were collected from the top aqueous layer. The resulting homogeneous yellow clear solution is rotovapoured at 55°C to remove dichloromethane and further it is vacuum dried. The obtained solid product is highly water soluble which is redispersed in water and stored at 4°C for further use. The attracting feature of this route is that neither any chemicals nor post-synthetic surface passivation. The starting material can be obtained from a single natural source and the synthesis method is cost-effective for large scale production.

The proposed mechanism for the formation of TCDs from tamarind (Scheme 1 ) is carbonization of the constituents of tamarind which mainly consist of sugar (12.5%), tartaric acid (5–8%), citric acid (0.64–3.95%), potassium tartrate (4.5–6%), malic, formic, acetic and butyric acids (trace amounts). Sugar is short-chain soluble carbohydrate which is mainly composed of carbon, hydrogen and oxygen. When sugar undergoes hydrothermal treatment convert into glucose and fructose which further decomposed into different soluble products like furfural compounds. Also the presence of acetic, malic, formic and butyric acids in tamarind undergoes polymerisation and condensation in a controlled manner and gives rise to soluble polymers. Formation of aromatic clusters of these soluble polymers leads nucleation of carbon dots.

HR-TEM images in Fig.1 a demonstrate the TCDs prepared are nearly quasi-spherical and mono-dispersed with an average diameter of 1–3nm displayed in histogram (Fig. S1). The inset of Fig.1a is the HR-TEM image in 5μm scale which discloses the prepared TCDs is spherical in shape. AFM and DLS analysis also reveals that the synthesized TCDs are in nanometer scale (Figs. S2, S3). The SEM image (Fig.1b) of TCDs emerge like layered structures of graphene oxide sheets and the EDAS (Fig.1c) analysis expose that TCDs is mainly consist of C (62.6%) and O (37.2%) which clearly illustrate that the TCDs are made up of oxygenous carbon structure. XRD pattern of the TCDs is represented in Fig.1d, which exhibit two sharp diffraction peaks at 2θ =20°, 38° and also show small diffraction peaks at 2θ =48° and 58° is attributed to carbon composed in a considerably random fashion. This result indicate that the carbon atoms exist as densely packed alkyl chains with graphitic carbons which enlightening the poor crystalline nature of CDs (amorphous in nature) is due to more number of oxygen containing groups present in the surface of TCDs.

XPS analysis is performed to investigate the composition and surface state of TCDs. The survey scan of XPS spectrum (Fig.2 a, Table S1) exposes two prominent features of C1s and O1s located at 284 and 533eV respectively. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum (Fig. S4) reveals four distinct peaks located at 284.2, 285.3, 286.7 and 289.2eV, which are ascribed to CC, COC, CO and OCO groups, respectively. Similarly the typical O1s spectrum (Fig. S5) can be resolute into two peaks at 529.7 and 532.9eV, which are attributed to CO and COH/COC bands respectively. The XPS results are consistent with the FT-IR analysis of TCDs shown in Fig.2b. The peaks around 1255cm−1 and 1129cm−1 are assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COC [20]. Peaks associated with the stretching vibrations of carboxylic (COO) and hydroxyl (OH) groups are at 1735 and 3435cm−1. The characteristic peaks at 2925cm−1, 2852cm−1 are due to the stretching vibrations of sp2 and sp3 CH. The peak at 1625cm−1 corresponds to the CC stretching vibration. The FT-IR data revealing the formation of unsaturated carbon during the carbonization process and the presence of oxygen-rich groups (carboxyl, carbonyl and hydroxyl) on the surface [21] which imparts the excellent water soluble nature of TCDs. Zeta potential measurement (Fig. S6) revealed that TCDs has a value of −7.34mV, which indicates the existence of negatively charged carboxyl groups on the surface of TCDs and the surface is hydrophilic in nature which enhances the high solubility of TCDs in water. The NMR spectra disclose the presence of four different kinds of chemical environments in the four different regions as discussed below. In the 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. S7) the regions found are: 1–3ppm (for sp3 CH protons), 3–5ppm (for the protons attached with hydroxyl, ether and carbonyl groups), 5–8ppm (for the aromatic or sp2 protons) and 8–9ppm (for the aldehydic protons). Also in the 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. S8), four similar regions viz. 20–40ppm (for sp3 carbons and carbons attached with hydroxyl groups), 60–85ppm (for carbons attached with ether linkages), 125–135ppm (for aromatic or sp2 carbons) and 170–180ppm (for CO carbons) were found. The NMR data are consistent with the literature report [22]. The characterization results from FT-IR, XPS and NMR were consistent to each other which confirms the various functional groups present on the surface of TCDs will make it better towards diverse applications.

Fig. S9 shows the absorption and emission spectra of prepared TCDs in which the absorption spectrum exhibits a sharp peak at 280nm is due to the π–π∗ transitions in the aromatic CC bond in the core and a broad shoulder at 330nm is due to n–π∗ transitions in the CO bond originate from the surface of TCDs. Similar transitions were also observed in the excitation spectrum of TCDs (Fig. S10). TCDs exhibits maximum emission at the wavelength of 442nm while excitation at 360nm. The emission arises from the various defects present on the surface sites of carbon dots. The quantum yield for TCDs is found to be 4% which is consistent with the literature reported for carbon dots prepared via green routes. Fig.2c shows the excitation dependent emission spectra of TCDs. When the excitation wavelength varied from 300 to 460nm in 20nm increments, it is observed that emission maxima varies from blue to green region (380–520nm), this bathochromic shift in emission wavelength with decrease in fluorescence intensity is attributed to the different functional groups such as ketonic, carbonyl and hydroxyl groups present in the surface of carbon dots, which is consistent with the FT-IR measurement. Here, it is noted that the excitation dependent emission may be associated with the anti-stokes emission of TCDs and surface energy trap is thought to be the reason for the strong emission. This result is consistent with the previous reports for carbon dots from green synthesis [23]. The fluorescence decay of TCDs is shown in Fig.2d. The decay is fitted by a multiexponential function [F(t)=A1 exp (−t/τ1)+A2 exp (−t/τ2)+A3 exp (−t/τ3)] with the lifetimes of τ1 =1.01ns (35%), τ2 =3.11ns (41%), τ3 =8.32ns (24%) and the average lifetime calculated as τav =3.62ns. The multiple lifetimes of the fluorescence imply that various emissive states, which is due to a wide range of chemical environments on the surface of carbon dots.

Before employing TCDs to diverse applications, it is necessary to check the stability by various parameters such as effect of pH, NaCl and irradiation time on the fluorescence intensity of TCDs which are given in (Figs. S11–S13). From the effect of various parameters we could found that the prepared TCDs are sensitive with pH of the medium. Moreover, the emission intensity of TCDs is high at the physiological pH which is good sign to use TCDs in biological applications. The fluorescence intensity of TCDs is not much varied by adding NaCl (0.1–1M) and irradiation time (0–100min), which clearly reveals that the prepared carbon dots are capable of outstanding stability and tolerance without any post stabilization treatments.

Photoinduced electron transfer properties of carbon based nanomaterials may offer new opportunities for light energy conversion and related applications such as in photovoltaics [25,26]. Therefore, it is necessary to study the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) characteristics of the prepared TCDs. Hence, here we have selected a well known electron acceptor (methyl viologen, MV) which has a similar reduction potential (−0.44V) when compared to the TiO2 conduction band. If the electron transfer takes place between TCDs and MV, one can assume that the same process will be operate to TiO2.

Fig.3 a shows the time resolved fluorescence decay of TCDs with varying MV concentration (0–5× 10−3 M) excited at 375nm. From this figure, it is clear that the fluorescence decay of TCDs is regularly quenched by increasing concentration MV. The quenching of fluorescence decay is due to decrease in fluorescence lifetime. The, decrease in fluorescence lifetime can be analyzed by using Stern–Volmer relationship (2) [27]:
(2)
τ
0
/ τ = 1 + Ksv
[ Q ]
where τ0 and τ are the fluorescence lifetime of TCDs in the absence and presence of methyl viologen and KSV is the quenching constant. The plot between τ0/τ and [Q] is linear (Fig.3b), it indicates that dynamic nature of quenching is observed in this system. From the slope of the linear plot, the value of KSV is calculated as 46M−1. The quenching rate constant (kq) is calculated with the help of average lifetime (τ0) of TCDs (3.62ns). By using the equation kq =KSV/τ0, the value of kq is 1.27× 1010 M−1 s−1. The obtained kq value is in the order of 1010 which is close to the diffusion-controlled rate constant limit, which clearly suggest that, dynamic quenching plays a major role in the fluorescence quenching of TCDs by MV. Moreover, the obtained quenching rate constant for TCDs-MV system is very similar with previously reported quenching rate constant of CDs-nitro toluene for electron transfer reactions [15]. Hence, we assigned the quenching of TCDs by MV is due to electron transfer. The mechanism for the quenching of TCDs by the acceptor is due to retardation of electron and hole recombination [28]. Further, in order to prove our hypothesis (i.e.) electron donating ability of TCDs, we have performed fluorescence quenching measurements with hydroxy-TEMPO under the similar experimental conditions. It is previously reported that, hydroxy-TEMPO can able to accept the electron from CDs [29]. As like MV, the hydroxy-TEMPO also quenches the fluorescence of TCDs. Stern-volmer plot for the quenching of hydroxy-TEMPO also linear (Fig.3b). The calculated kq is 1.04× 1010 M−1 s−1, which is also close to the diffusion-controlled rate constant limit. Based on the results, we believed that TCDs can be act as electron donor material, thus offering opportunities for their potential uses in light energy conversion and related applications.

In conclusion, we have reported a one step hydrothermal route for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from a daily use Indian kitchen material (tamarind) as a precursor without any further post synthesis stabilization treatments. The prepared carbon dots exhibit excellent water solubility, storage stability and 4% quantum yield with the particle size of 1–3nm. The carbon dots are pH sensitive with negatively charged functional groups present on the surface. The prepared TCDs exhibit the unique nature of excitation dependent emission with the average lifetime of 3.62ns. Moreover, electron transfer quenching of TCDs with acceptors is demonstrated. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching of TCDs by acceptors is due to retardation of electron hole recombination. The other applications of TCDs such as in sensors, metal ion detection and bioimaging are in under progress.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: M. A. J and S. T thanks to DST-SERB, India for the project and fellowship respectively [Ref. No. SB/FT/CS-125/2013, Dt. 30/06/2014]. M.A.J thanks Nano-IITM and SAIF, IIT Madras for assistance with HR-TEM and SEM facilities. M.A.J. also acknowledges Prof. G.U. Kulkarni, Ms. Selvi and Ms. Kiruthika for EDAS and power XRD analysis. We also thanks to National Centre for Ultrafast processes, University of Madras for lifetime measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2016.08.081.

ce:e-component.ce:label: Supplementary Figs. S1–S13 and Table S1

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Supplementary material contains Figs. S1–S13 and Table S1.
